title: Känguru der Mathematik 2017 — Felix (Pré-Écolier, Grades 1-2)
source_organization: Känguru der Mathematik (Austria) / Kangourou sans Frontières
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper (kaenguru.at, via matematica.pt). For personal educational practice only.
answer_source: Official 2017_Answers.pdf answer table (Felix row), read and verified.

questions.question_text: Ellen wants to decorate the butterfly using these 6 stickers (two light circles, two thin dark ovals, two dark circles). Which butterfly can she make?
questions.solution: The butterfly's two wings are mirror images, so the stickers must be placed the same way on both sides using exactly the 6 given stickers. Only butterfly A does this.
solution_steps: The two wings must match like a mirror.
Use exactly the 6 given stickers, the same on each wing.
Only butterfly A fits, so A.

questions.question_text: Into how many pieces will the string be cut?
questions.solution: Each time the scissors' dashed line crosses the string it makes one more cut. The line crosses the looped string 8 times, splitting it into 9 pieces.
solution_steps: Count where the dashed cutting line crosses the string.
It crosses 8 times.
8 cuts split the string into 8 + 1 = 9 pieces.

questions.question_text: How many blocks are missing in this igloo?
questions.solution: Count the empty spaces in the igloo wall where blocks should be. There are 10 gaps, so 10 blocks are missing.
solution_steps: Look at the white gap in the igloo.
Count how many block-sized spaces are empty.
There are 10 missing blocks.

questions.question_text: This picture shows a bracelet with pearls. Which of the bands below shows the same bracelet?
questions.solution: Unroll the round bracelet into a line, keeping the pearl colours in their circular order. The band that matches that order (in either direction) is option E.
solution_steps: Imagine cutting the round bracelet and laying it flat.
Keep the pearls in their circular order.
The matching straight band is option E.

questions.question_text: Four of the numbers 1, 3, 4, 5, 7 are written into the boxes so that □ + □ = □ + □ is correct. Which number was NOT used?
questions.solution: The four used numbers split into two equal sums, so their total is even. All five numbers add to 20 (even), so the unused number must also be even. The only even number is 4.
solution_steps: The two sides are equal, so the four used numbers total an even number.
All five numbers add to 20 (even), so the leftover is even too.
The only even number is 4, so 4 is unused (e.g. 1+7 = 3+5).

questions.question_text: Jim and Ben are sitting in a Ferris wheel. After it turns, Ben is in the position where Jim was before. Where is Jim now?
questions.solution: Ben moved into Jim's old seat, so the wheel turned by the gap between their seats. Turning Jim's seat by that same amount lands him in position C.
solution_steps: The wheel turned by the gap from Ben's old seat to Jim's old seat.
Apply that same turn to Jim's seat.
Jim ends up in position C.

questions.question_text: Alfred turns his building block 10 times. The first three turns are shown. What is the final position of the building block?
questions.solution: The turning repeats in a cycle. Following the pattern of rotations through all 10 turns lands the block in the position shown in option D.
solution_steps: The turns repeat in a regular cycle.
Carry the pattern on from turn 3 up to turn 10.
The final position is option D.

questions.question_text: In which picture are there half as many circles as triangles and twice as many squares as triangles?
questions.solution: Let the number of triangles be the key. Circles must be half that and squares double that — for example 2 triangles, 1 circle, 4 squares. The picture with these counts is option E.
solution_steps: Circles = half the triangles; squares = twice the triangles.
So a working set is 1 circle, 2 triangles, 4 squares.
The picture with those counts is option E.

questions.question_text: Leo and Max are in a queue of 11 people. There are 7 people in front of Leo, and Max stands directly behind Leo. How many people are behind Max?
questions.solution: With 7 people in front, Leo is 8th and Max is 9th. Out of 11 people, positions 10 and 11 are behind Max — that is 2 people.
solution_steps: 7 in front of Leo → Leo is 8th, Max is 9th.
People behind Max are positions 10 and 11.
That is 2 people.

questions.question_text: Old McDonald has a horse, two cows and three pigs. How many more cows does he need so that exactly half of all his animals are cows?
questions.solution: He has 6 animals now, with 2 cows. Adding c cows gives 6 + c animals and 2 + c cows. For cows to be half: 2 + c = (6 + c)/2, which gives c = 2.
solution_steps: Now: 6 animals, 2 cows.
Add c cows: cows = 2 + c, total = 6 + c, and 2 + c must be half of 6 + c.
Solving gives c = 2 more cows.

questions.question_text: Every box shows the sum of the number on the very left and the number on the very top (for example 5 + 7 = 12). Which number is written behind the star?
questions.solution: The box showing 14 is in the '?' row under the 10 column, so ? + 10 = 14, giving ? = 4. The star is ? + 7 = 4 + 7 = 11.
solution_steps: The 14 box means ? + 10 = 14, so ? = 4.
The star is in the same row under the 7 column.

questions.question_text: Lisa makes 7 identical crowns. From two kinds of construction-paper sheets she cuts out the parts. What is the minimum number of sheets she has to cut up?
questions.solution: Count how many dots, pluses and lines all 7 crowns need, then see how many of each sheet supply those parts. Choosing the fewest sheets that still cover every part needs 9 sheets.
solution_steps: Work out the total dots, pluses and lines for 7 crowns.
See how many parts each kind of sheet provides.
The smallest number of sheets covering all parts is 9.

questions.question_text: Simon has two identical tiles whose fronts look like an L-shape with one black square; the backs are white. Which pattern can he make with the two tiles?
questions.solution: Each tile may be turned or flipped (the back is plain white). Trying the allowed moves, the two tiles fit together to make pattern A.
solution_steps: Each tile can be rotated or flipped over (white back).
Try fitting the two tiles to match each pattern.
They make pattern A.

questions.question_text: A kangaroo does ten jumps within a minute, then takes a three-minute break. How many minutes does it need to do 50 jumps?
questions.solution: Each set is 10 jumps in 1 minute, so 50 jumps needs 5 jumping minutes. Between them are 4 breaks of 3 minutes = 12 minutes. The 5th jumping minute finishes the 50th jump at minute 4 + 12 + 1 = 17.
solution_steps: 50 jumps = 5 jumping minutes (10 jumps each).
Between the 5 jumping minutes are 4 breaks of 3 min = 12 min.
Total to finish the last jump = 5 + 12 = 17 minutes.

questions.question_text: Each key locks exactly one padlock; each letter stands for one digit (same letters = same digits). The padlocks read ADA, DGA, DAG and ?, with keys 141, 717, 417, 471. Which letters are on the fourth padlock?
questions.solution: ADA has the form X-Y-X, matching 141, so A = 1, D = 4. Then DGA = 471 gives G = 7, and DAG = 417 checks out. The leftover key 717 is 7-1-7 = G-A-G, so the fourth padlock reads GAG.
solution_steps: ADA = X-Y-X matches 141: A = 1, D = 4.
DGA = 471 → G = 7; DAG = 417 fits too.
Leftover key 717 = G-A-G, so the padlock is GAG.
